figures.caption: Table 4: Sensitivity of the RL stage to its main design choices. We report final best costs on 100 uniform instances with n = 100. We also report the relative training cost under a fixed number of parameter-update steps. The default configuration used in the final model is highlighted in bold.

Table 3: Additional disentangled ablations of the final method. Results are reported on the main validation setting using the same evaluation protocol as in the main text on 100 uniform instances with n = 100. The default configuration used in the final model is highlighted in bold.

Table 1: Best tour cost and inference time under a fixed budget of 10n improvement steps for search methods, and a single forward pass for constructive methods. Bold indicates the best cost and fastest time among learned improvement methods.

Figure 1: Overview of the edge-based representation and policy architecture. Tours are represented as sequences of edge tokens, each encoding endpoint coordinates and local geometric features. These edge tokens are processed by an edge-attention encoder with local cycle mixing and global context fusion, producing contextualized edge representations. A pairwise decoder then scores all feasible 2-opt moves.
Figure 5: Ablation study. Each row adds one component. Bars show optimality (%).

Figure 2: Overview of the proposed two-stage learning framework. An imitation learning stage uses optimal short-horizon trajectories, followed by a group-based reinforcement learning stage that refines the policy through instance-wise competition in longer horizons.
